Documentation for running a Flask application with a development server, including CLI usage and error handling.

# Purpose
This documentation provides instructions for using the Flask development server. It explains how to run the server using the `flask run` command, which is suitable for local development and debugging. The document warns against using the development server in production due to its lack of efficiency, stability, and security. It also addresses common issues such as port conflicts, providing solutions like changing the port or stopping the conflicting process. Additionally, it describes how to handle errors during development, including deferred errors on reload and immediate errors on initial run. The document also covers starting the server programmatically using the `Flask.run` method in Python, highlighting differences from the command-line interface.
# Content Summary
The provided content is documentation for running a Flask application using a development server. It explains the use of the `flask run` command, which is the recommended method to start the development server. The command can be configured with the `--app` option to specify the application and the `--debug` option to enable debug mode. Debug mode provides an interactive debugger and automatically reloads the server when code changes occur.

The documentation warns against using the development server in production due to its lack of efficiency, stability, and security. For production deployment, users are directed to other documentation.

If the default port 5000 is already in use, the documentation provides guidance on resolving the issue. Users can either stop the conflicting process or choose a different port using the `--port` option. It provides examples of using `netstat` and `lsof` to identify the process using the port.

The document also addresses deferred errors on reload, explaining that the server will continue running even if errors are introduced after it starts. However, if errors exist when the server is initially started, it will fail immediately and display a traceback.

Additionally, the documentation describes how to start the development server programmatically using the `Flask.run` method in Python. This method accepts similar arguments to the CLI options, but the server will crash on reload errors. It is recommended to place the call within a main block to avoid interference with production server imports.

Overall, the document provides essential instructions for developers to effectively use the Flask development server during local development.
